text:
Admission Date:  1978-05-23              Discharge Date:   1979-06-28

Date of Birth:  1972-01-21             Sex:   F

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Fosamax / Zyvox / Heparin Agents / Statins-Hmg-Coa Reductase
Inhibitors

Attending:Marks
Chief Complaint:
hypoxemia

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
none

History of Present Illness:
Dr. Neal is an 80 yo retired psychiatrist with h/o copd, afib,
recent micu admission for pna. Sent from NH for hypoxemia.
Vitals in the ED were 0 98 86 122/74 24 84% 6L NC. She had a
large right sided pneumonia on CXR. Her ECG showed afib with
rates 80-110. She was given cipro, vanco, ceftriaxone as well as
nebs and steroids. On transfer HR 96 96/60, 26 96% 4L.
.
She states that her shortness of breath started this morning.
She has had some wheezing but no cough. She denies chest pain,
orthopnea, PND and LE edema. She denies fever. She had been
getting nebs but there were stopped several days ago. She is on
a prednisone taper. She has been in afib and usually does not
feel palpitations but she states she did feel palpitations last
night.


Past Medical History:
- CAD with h/o anterior MI s/p PCI
- CHF (LVEF 2010 40-45%)
- HTN
- Atrial fibrillation
- H/o CVA w/o residual deficits
- COPD
- Spinal stenosis with both leg weakness wheelchair bound
- Breast cancer
- Leg weakness
- Peripheral artery disease
- Osteoarthritis
- PUD


Social History:
She is a retired psychiatrist. She lives alone, HHA twice
weekly. Uses a wheelchair, former smoker - 150 pk-yrs, quit in
2006.


Family History:
Significant for hypertension and history of arrhythmias in her
mother. Stroke in both mother and father. Father had asthma.

GENERAL: Elderly woman in NAD. Oriented x3.
HEENT: NCAT. Sclera anicteric. PERRL, EOMI. Conjunctiva were
pink, no pallor or cyanosis of the oral mucosa. No xanthalesma.

NECK: Supple. JVP elevated to mid-neck.
CARDIAC: Irregular, normal S1, S2. No m/r/g.

wheezes diffusely.
ABDOMEN: Soft, NTND. No HSM or tenderness. Abd aorta not
enlarged by palpation. No abdominial bruits.
EXTREMITIES: No c/c/e. No femoral bruits.
SKIN: +Venous stasis changes. +Scattered ecchymoses. No edema.

Right: Carotid 2+ Femoral 2+ DP 2+ PT 2+
Left: Carotid 2+ Femoral 2+ DP 2+ PT 2+

LUNGS: Respirations unlabored. Diminished breath sounds
diffusely.  Dullness to percusion at R base
ABDOMEN: Soft, NTND. No HSM or tenderness.
EXTREMITIES:  Cool extremities, 2+ PE
SKIN: +Venous stasis changes. +Scattered ecchymoses. No edema.

ECG 1978-05-23:
Atrial fibrillation. Right bundle-branch block. Left anterior
fascicular
block. Anterior myocardial infarction of undetermined age.
Cannot exclude
ischemia. Clinical correlation is suggested. Since the previous
tracing
of 2005-04-09 precordial lead ST-T wave changes appear slightly more
prominent
but may be no significant change.

Portable CXR 1978-05-23:
IMPRESSION: Overall stable radiograph with small bilateral
pleural effusions and presumed atelectasis at the lung bases.
The opacity at the right lung base, however, is slightly more
confluent and an underlying infiltrate cannot be entirely
excluded.

TTE 1986-05-08:
The left atrium is dilated. The right atrium is moderately
dilated. There is mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy
with normal cavity size. There is mild regional left ventricular
systolic dysfunction with hypokinesis of the distal anterior
wall and septum. There is no ventricular septal defect. Right
ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal. The
aortic valve leaflets (3) are mildly thickened but aortic
stenosis is not present. No aortic regurgitation is seen. The
mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. Mild (1+) mitral
regurgitation is seen. The tricuspid valve leaflets are mildly
thickened. There is mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension.
There is no pericardial effusion.

Compared with the report of the prior study (images unavailable
for review) of 2009-05-02, pulmonary artery pressures can be seen
to be mildly elevated on the current study. The other findings
are similar.

Video Swallow 2000-09-30:
FINDINGS:
Barium passes freely through the oropharynx and esophagus
without evidence of obstruction. There is no gross aspiration.
Several episodes of penetration were noted during administration
of thin consistency barium. For details, please refer to speech
and swallow division note in OMR.

IMPRESSION:
No evidence of aspiration. Several episodes of penetration with
thin liquids.

CT chest w/o contrast 1970-05-26:
FINDINGS: Bilateral new nonhemorrhagic moderately large pleural
effusions
have developed since 1986. There is no pathologic enlargement of
the
supraclavicular, mediastinal or axillary lymph nodes. Mild
cardiac
enlargement predominantly affects both atria, without associated
pericardial effusion. Dense calcification of the mitral annulus
is present and appears relatively unchanged since 2002-10-31.
Aortic annulus and coronary artery calcifications are mild.
Atherosclerotic calcification of the aortic arch and descending
thoracic aorta are moderately severe. Respiratory secretions in
the trachea are small in volume. The lower trachea has a
saber-sheath configuration consistent with chronic obstructive
airways disease.Collapse of the bronchus intermedius is
moderately severe (2:23).

Centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema is severe and has
progressed since
2002-10-31. Right lower lobe atelectasis is extensive and related
in part to a moderately large hiatal hernia. Allowing for the
limitation of significant motion artifact in both lower lobes,
there is no evidence of pulmonary nodules. Left lower lobe
atelectasis is likely secondary to the moderately large left
pleural effusion.

The study is not tailored for subdiaphragmatic evaluation, only
to confirm
normal-appearing adrenals bilaterally, a moderately large hiatal
hernia which contains most of the stomach and multiple
low-attenuation lesions in the liver, the largest of which lies
in the segment Gary which measures 14 x 23 mm and has enlarged
since 2002-10-31 when it measured 9 x 14 mm (2:47), moderately
severe atherosclerotic calcification of a tortuous abdominal
aorta and its branches, a new right upper renal low-attenuation
lesion (2:48) and oral contrast in the small bowel from a video
swallow dated 2000-09-30.

Thoracolumbar levoscoliosis and marked deformity of the left
humeral head,
likely relate to prior fracture are unchanged but collapse of a
lower thoracic vertebrae with 30% loss of height of T12
(401B:34), has progressed since 2002-10-31.

IMPRESSION:
1. Worsening severe centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema.
2. Saber-sheath tracheal configuration and subtotal collapse of
the bronchus intermedius suggesting tracheobronchomalacia.
3. New moderately large bilateral pleural effusions. Associated
bilateral
bibasilar atelectasis.
4. New right renal low-attenuation upper pole lesion and
enlarging liver
lesions. If ultrasound evaluation has not been performed, it
would be the
next appropriate step.

Brief Hospital Course:
80yo retired psychiatrist with h/o COPD brought in from nursing
home with hypoxemia.
.
# Hypoxemia
She was initially treated for pneumonia based on her chest xray
RLL consolidation with antibiotics to cover for HCAP organisms
(vanc/cefepime/cipro). Her history was more consistent with CHF
with sudden onset shortness of breath and absence of fever,
cough or sputum. Antibiotics were initially discontinued after
24hrs.  A later chest x-ray on 1992-02-03 was more suggestive of
PNA in the right lower and left lower lobes. Her antibiotics
were restarted; she was placed on vanc/[**Date Range **]/flagyl given her
history of MRSA and risk of aspiration as noted by a video
swallow exam.  A high resolution CT chest was obtained for
further evaluation of her multiple respiratory issues.  The CT
chest did not show pneumonia and her antibiotics were again
discontinued.  Her hypoxemia was believed to be due to a
combination of her COPD and acute on chronic CHF exacerbation.
She had initially been on a prednisone taper on admission; her
steroid dose was increased to 60mg on this admission.  She
remained on 60mg daily for several days and was tapered slowly,
decreasing by 5mg each day, as her respiratory status became
more stable.  Oxygen saturation remained in the 90s on room air
or on 1-2L oxygen.  Given her severe COPD, goal oxygen
saturation was believed to be 88-92%RA.  She was also diuresed
with IV lasix during admission and placed on po torsemide on
discharge.  Her weight one day prior to discharge was 52.4 kg.

.
# CHF, acte on chronic, systolic and diastolic: Pt with EF
40-45%.  In the ICU, she was diuresed on a lasix gtt and was net
negative 3.5L.  On the floor, she was given prn doses of 20mg iv
lasix.  Diuresis was limited by hypotension (BP ranged from 80s
to 110s systolic).  CT chest showed moderate b/l pleural
effusions; she was discharged on po torsemide.  Her lisinopril
was discontinued given her hyperkalemia and hypotension.
- She will need her I/O and daily weights followed.
.
# Metabolic alkalosis: Pt with bicarb in the 40s, peaking at 48.
 This was likely multifactorial; hypercarbia was primarily
believed to be 01-24 to her severe COPD.  Pt may also have
developed a contraction alkalosis from diuresis.
.
# Atrial fibrillation: She was continued on amiodarone.
Metoprolol was initially reduced 37.5mg QID to 37.5mg TID given
her low normal BPs.  Heart rates were suboptimally controlled
and her beta blocker was again increased to QID by time of
discharge.  HR ranged from 70s-100s.  BP ranged systolic 80s to
110s during hospital admission; she was asymptomatic at times of
low BPs.  On admission, coumadin was initially held given
supratherapeutic INR and reintiated when INR was 2.3.  She was
briefly started on lovenox for concern that INR would fall below
2.  This was discontinued when INR remained stable between 2 and
3.  Upon initiation of [**Month/Day (2) **] and flagyl for ?PNA, INR rose to 5.2
despite the fact that coumadin dose had been reduced.  Coumadin
was again held on discharge; INR was 4.4 on discharge.  She will
need close monitoring of INR at rehab.  Pt was again seen by EP
while in the hospital and an outpatient cardioversion was
planned within 1 week of discharge.
.
# CAD: She was continued on her aspirin and metoprolol.  She had
a recorded allergy to statins.  Lisinopril was discontinued
given her hyperkalemia and hypotension.
.
# COPD: Pt had been on a prednisone taper when admitted; her
steroid dose was increased to 60mg as COPD exacerbation was
major component of her hypoxia.  She was kept on 60mg for
several days until resp status stabilized.  She was then
gradually tapered, decreasing by 5mg daily.  A high resolution
CT chest was obtained to better evaluate her several respiratory
complaints.  This showed worsening severe cetrilobular and
paraseptal emphysema, collapse of the bronchus intermedius
suggestive of tracheobronchomalacia, and new moderately large
b/l pleural effusions.  Per discussion with radiology, there
were no signs of amiodarone-induced toxicity on CT.  Pulmonary
consult was initiated but pt refused the consult.  Discussion
was held regarding the indications for pulmonary consult
considering these concerning CT findings; pt stated
understanding of risks and benefits and refused the consult.
She reported that she had seen an outpatient pulmonologist in
the past who she did not find helpful.  She refused outpatient
pulmonary follow-up as well.
.
# Liver/Kidney lesions: High resolution CT chest incidentally
showed a new right renal low attenuation upper pole lesion as
well as enlarging liver lesions.  An abdominal ultrasound was
ordered to further assess these lesions.  Pt refused the
ultrasound, stating that she would not agree to intervention
regardless of what these lesions were.  Again, risks and
benefits of pursuing the ultrasound were discussed; pt stated
understanding and continued to decline the procedure.  LFTs were
within normal range.
.
# h/o peptic ulcer disease: She was continued on home
omeprazole.
.
# hypothyroidism: She was continued on home levothyroxine.  TSH
was normal at 1.9.
.
# ?aspiration: Speech and swallow assessment was obtained.  Pt
had mild oral and pharyngeal dysphagia. She was found to
penetrate thin liquids with risk of aspiration but was aware of
the aspiration and was able to cough when it occurred.  Per
video swallow report, aspiration events were infrequent and she
was advised to perform a chin tuck to minimize the risk.  She
was continued on a regular diet with thin liquids.

Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Sleepy Eye Medical Center Hospital - Rangely District Hospital

Discharge Diagnosis:
COPD exacerbation
Acute on chronic systolic CHF
Atrial fibrillation with RVR
Pleural effusions


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Out of Bed with assistance to chair or
wheelchair.


Discharge Instructions:
It was a pleasure taking care of you in the hospital.  You were
admitted with shortness of breath and a low oxygen level.  You
were initially admitted to the intensive care unit and started
on antibiotics because your chest x-ray showed a possible
pneumonia.  Your steroids were also increased to treat a COPD
exacerbation and you were diuresed to remove fluid in your
lungs.  Your heart rate was controlled with metoprolol and
amiodarone.  You were monitored carefully on warfarin; this was
held on discharge because your levels were supratherapeutic.
You will be contactJoel Morgan regarding an outpatient appointment for
cardioversion for your atrial fibrillation.

The following changes were made to your medications:
1) Your prednisone was initially increased to 60mg daily and
then tapered in the following way: decrease by 5mg and stay on
each new dose for two days:
2010-05-20 40mg;
2010-07-23 35mg;
1989-05-07 30mg;
2015-09-06 25mg;
2002-05-24 20mg;
1971-04-27 15mg;
2002-02-26 10mg;
2015-04-20 5mg
2) Your coumadin was briefly held because your INR levels were
high; your INR will be monitored daily and your coumadin
restarted accordingly
3) You were started on torsemide 20 mg oral daily; your blood
pressure should be monitored closely while you are on this
medication

Weigh yourself every morning, Misty Bishop MD if weight goes up more
than 3 lbs.

Followup Instructions:
You will be called by electrophysiology regarding an appointment
for cardioversion.
